title: Serving Two Markets: Tourists and Locals
subtitle: The Old Town tourist and the Žvėrynas regular need different things from you.

sections.heading: The Dual Customer Challenge
sections.body:
If your cafe is in Vilnius Old Town — the UNESCO-listed historic centre — you are serving two fundamentally different markets. In summer, 60% or more of your customers may be tourists: Germans, Scandinavians, Poles, Brits, Americans. In winter, that drops to 10–15%, and your survival depends on the locals.

If your cafe is in a residential neighbourhood — Žvėrynas, Antakalnis, Šnipiškės, Fabijoniškės — you serve locals year-round with a small tourist trickle in summer.

The challenge: tourists and locals want different things, pay differently, and evaluate your cafe by different criteria. A cafe that optimises for tourists risks alienating locals (who keep you alive in winter). A cafe that optimises for locals may miss the summer tourist revenue that funds the quieter months.

The answer is not choosing one over the other. It is understanding each segment clearly and designing your operation to serve both — without compromising on what makes you distinctive.

The first step is knowing your actual split. Check your POS data: what percentage of transactions are card payments from foreign banks? What is your revenue in July versus January? The gap tells you how dependent you are on each segment.

sections.heading: What Tourists Want
sections.body:
Tourists visiting Vilnius are increasingly sophisticated — many are traveling specifically for food and coffee experiences. They are not looking for generic; they are looking for authentic.

**Speed and convenience.** Tourists are on a schedule — sightseeing, tours, flights. They want to order quickly, pay easily (card or mobile, not cash), and receive their drink without a long wait. A queue of 10 minutes that a local tolerates is a skip for a tourist.

**English-language experience.** Menu in English (or at minimum bilingual). Staff who can explain items in English. Wifi password visible without asking. This is baseline, not a bonus.

**Instagram-worthy presentation.** Tourists document everything. A beautiful latte art, a photogenic interior, a well-plated pastry — these drive social media posts that market your cafe to their followers for free.

**Local authenticity.** The best tourist cafes offer something distinctly Lithuanian. Šakotis alongside the croissant. Varškės sūrelis on the counter. A Lithuanian-roasted single origin. Tourists want to taste the place they are visiting, not a copy of what they have at home.

**Willingness to pay premium.** A tourist from Stockholm or Munich finds Lithuanian specialty coffee remarkable value. They will pay €5 for a pour-over without blinking. This does not mean you should price-gouge — but you should not underprice either.

**Recommendations.** "What should I try?" is the most common tourist question. Train your team to answer it with enthusiasm and specificity: "Try the šaltibarščiai — it's a cold beetroot soup, totally Lithuanian, and it's perfect on a day like today."

sections.heading: What Locals Want
sections.body:
Local regulars are the foundation of any sustainable cafe. They visit 3–5 times a week, they tell friends, they leave reviews, and they keep you alive from October to April when the tourists are gone.

**Consistency.** The local who comes every morning wants the same flat white, made the same way, at the same quality. Surprise is not a feature for a regular — reliability is.

**Recognition.** Being known by name. Having their order remembered. Being greeted warmly. These micro-interactions create belonging. A regular who feels like a stranger in their own cafe will find another one.

**Fair pricing.** Locals compare your prices to every other cafe in their daily life. They notice a €0.30 increase. They compare your avocado toast to the one three streets away. Price fairly — and if you raise prices, do it with quality that justifies it.

**Lithuanian language.** This seems obvious but matters. If your team switches to English the moment a tourist walks in and forgets to switch back, the local at the next table feels deprioritised. The local language is the default; English is the accommodation.

**Quiet workspace.** Many Vilnius locals work remotely — IT, design, freelancing. A cafe with reliable wifi, power outlets, and a culture that welcomes laptop workers captures this segment. They buy 2–3 drinks in a 4-hour sitting. The revenue per hour is lower than a tourist turnover, but the frequency is daily.

**Community.** Locals want to feel part of something. Events, loyalty programmes, knowing the owner, seeing the same faces. A cafe that builds community creates customers who are almost impossible for competitors to steal.

sections.heading: Menu and Pricing Strategy
sections.body:
Your menu should serve both segments without alienating either.

**Bilingual menu.** Lithuanian and English. This is non-negotiable in any tourist-accessible area. List items in Lithuanian first with English descriptions — this signals that you are a Lithuanian cafe that welcomes visitors, not a tourist trap.

**Lithuanian products as tourist attraction:**

| Item | What It Is | Why Tourists Love It |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| Šaltibarščiai | Cold beetroot soup | Unique, photogenic, refreshing in summer |
| Šakotis | Spit cake | Dramatic appearance, traditional, shareable |
| Varškės sūreliai | Curd cheese bars | Sweet, unfamiliar, snackable |
| Žemaičių blynai | Potato pancakes with meat | Hearty, traditional, affordable |
| Lithuanian honey | Raw, local varieties | Retail item, gift potential |
| Local craft beer | Lithuanian microbreweries | Growing scene, pairs with food |

These items differentiate you from every other coffee shop in Europe. A tourist can get a flat white anywhere — they cannot get šaltibarščiai anywhere.

**Pricing approach:**
- Core coffee menu: same price for everyone, at market rate
- Lithuanian specialty food items: price at value (tourists expect to pay for unique experiences)
- Seasonal specials: higher margin items during peak tourist months (June–August)
- Loyalty pricing: stamp cards or membership discounts that reward frequency (captures locals)

Do not create separate tourist and local pricing — it creates resentment and looks cynical. Instead, design a menu that naturally upsells to tourists (unique items, larger portions, pairing suggestions) while remaining accessible to locals (core items at fair prices).

sections.heading: Language and Service
sections.body:
Vilnius is multilingual. Your team needs to navigate this gracefully.

**Lithuanian:** The default. Every interaction starts in Lithuanian unless the customer initiates in another language. This respects local customers and signals that you are a Lithuanian establishment.

**English:** Essential for tourist areas. Every team member should be able to take an order, explain the menu, and make recommendations in English. In Vilnius, English proficiency is generally high among young people — but practise cafe-specific vocabulary: "Would you like milk alternatives?", "This is our house blend from a local roaster", "The šaltibarščiai is served cold — it's a traditional Lithuanian soup."

**Russian:** Still spoken by a segment of older customers and some tourists from neighbouring countries. The politics of Russian in Lithuania are sensitive — follow your customer's lead. If they speak Russian, respond in Russian if your team can. If they speak Lithuanian, respond in Lithuanian. Never assume.

**Polish:** Some tourists from Poland, and a local Polish-speaking minority. Basic politeness in Polish ("dziękuję", "proszę") goes a long way.

**Service switching rules:**
- Start every interaction in Lithuanian
- Switch to English if the customer speaks English or appears to be a tourist
- Return to Lithuanian immediately when serving the next local customer
- Never leave a local waiting while giving extended attention to a tourist in English — it signals that tourists are more important

**Signage:** Menu boards in Lithuanian and English. Wifi password on the table. Allergen information available in both languages. A simple "Welcome / Sveiki" at the door sets the bilingual tone immediately.

sections.heading: Seasonal Revenue Planning
sections.body:
The tourist-local dynamic creates dramatic seasonal swings that you must plan for.

**The Lithuanian cafe year:**

| Period | Tourist Level | Local Level | Revenue Index | Strategy |
|--------|-------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| Jan–Feb | Very low | Steady | 60–70% | Survive: reduce hours, cut costs, invest in training |
| Mar–Apr | Low | Steady | 75–85% | Build: spring menu, terrace prep, hiring for summer |
| May–Jun | Rising | Steady | 90–110% | Grow: terrace opens, extended hours, summer menu |
| Jul–Aug | Peak | Lower (holidays) | 120–150% | Harvest: maximum revenue, full staffing, tourist focus |
| Sep | Declining | Returning | 100–110% | Transition: summer wind-down, autumn menu |
| Oct–Nov | Low | Steady | 80–90% | Consolidate: winter menu, cosy atmosphere, local focus |
| Dec | Moderate (Christmas market) | High | 95–110% | Celebrate: Christmas specials, gift items, events |

**Financial planning:**
- July and August surplus must fund January and February deficit
- Build a cash reserve of 2–3 months operating costs by end of September
- Budget heating costs separately (€800–1,500/month October–April vs €200–300 May–September)
- Staff seasonally: hire 2–3 extra for June–August, return to core team October

**The January question:**
Should you close for 1–2 weeks in January? Many Vilnius cafes do. The maths often support it — if your daily revenue is €300 but your daily costs (staff + heat + rent allocation) are €350, you lose €50/day by staying open. A planned closure saves money, gives the team a real break, and allows deep cleaning and renovation.

Alternatively, reduce hours dramatically: open 10am–4pm, skeleton crew, winter-only menu. Test both approaches and measure.
